title: CPQ Exercises — Configuration Engine
description: Hands-on exercises covering configurable products, rules, formula builder, scripting, containers, and multi-level configuration.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Build a Configurable Product with Dependency Rules
sections.content:
Objective

Create a fully configurable product with at least 4 attributes, including dependency rules that hide, disallow, or force-select values based on other selections.

Scenario

You are configuring a Cloud Hosting Package with the following attributes:

  • Plan (User Selection): Starter, Professional, Enterprise
  • Region (User Selection): EU-West, EU-North, US-East, US-West, APAC
  • Storage (User Selection): 100GB, 500GB, 1TB, 5TB
  • Support (User Selection): Email Only, Business Hours, 24/7 Premium

Steps

  1. Create all 4 attributes centrally under Setup → Product Catalog → Attributes.
  2. Create a configurable product YourInitials_Cloud_Hosting with Base Price: 0 (pricing will come from attributes). Assign all 4 attributes as Required.
  3. Configure the layout: Plan → Region → Storage → Support.
  4. Add the following Simple Rules on the Rules and Messages tab:
    • Rule 1: If Plan = Starter → Disallow Storage 5TB (Starter plan cannot have 5TB).
    • Rule 2: If Plan = Starter → Disallow Support 24/7 Premium (not available on Starter).
    • Rule 3: If Plan = Enterprise → Force-select Support 24/7 Premium (Enterprise always includes premium support).
  5. Test each rule in Layout → Configuration Preview.

Bonus Challenge

Add a Formula Rule that shows a Product Message "APAC region requires Enterprise plan" when Region = APAC and Plan ≠ Enterprise. Use the Formula Builder with IfAnyValue for the condition and a message action to set the product as Incomplete.

Validation

Walk through every combination: select Starter and verify 5TB and 24/7 are blocked. Select Enterprise and verify 24/7 is auto-selected. Select APAC with Starter and verify the message appears. Confirm the product cannot be added to a quote while Incomplete.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Dynamic Part Number & Pricing Formulas
sections.content:
Objective

Use the Formula Builder to generate a dynamic part number from attribute selections and create a pricing formula that calculates the total price based on the configuration.

Scenario

Using the Cloud Hosting product from Exercise 1, implement dynamic part numbering and attribute-based pricing.

Steps

Part A — Dynamic Part Number

  1. Navigate to the product definition and click the Formula Builder icon next to the Part Number field.
  2. Build a formula that concatenates: CHP- + ValueCode of Plan + - + ValueCode of Region + - + ValueCode of Storage.
  3. Use <* ValueCode(Plan) *> syntax for each attribute.
  4. Click Check Syntax to validate.
  5. Save and test in Configuration Preview. Verify that selecting "Enterprise / EU-West / 1TB" generates a part number like CHP-ENT-EUW-1TB (adjust based on your value codes).

Part B — Pricing Formula

  1. Assign prices to each attribute value. On the product's Attributes tab, set custom prices:
    • Plan: Starter=99, Professional=249, Enterprise=599
    • Region: EU-West=0, EU-North=0, US-East=20, US-West=20, APAC=50
    • Storage: 100GB=0, 500GB=49, 1TB=99, 5TB=249
    • Support: Email Only=0, Business Hours=79, 24/7 Premium=199
  2. Verify the product's pricing type is Custom Pricing with Base Price: 0.
  3. The total should automatically be: Base(0) + Plan price + Region surcharge + Storage price + Support price.
  4. Test in Configuration Preview: Enterprise + APAC + 5TB + 24/7 Premium should total 599 + 50 + 249 + 199 = 1097.

Bonus Challenge

Create a Pricing Formula that applies a 10% discount when Plan = Enterprise, using:

<* Eval(
  [IF](<* IsSelected(Plan.Enterprise) *>)
  { (<* Price(Plan) *> + <* Price(Region) *> + <* Price(Storage) *> + <* Price(Support) *>) * 0.90 }
  { <* Price(Plan) *> + <* Price(Region) *> + <* Price(Storage) *> + <* Price(Support) *> }
  [ENDIF]
) *>

Verify the Enterprise total is discounted by 10%.

Validation

Test at least 4 different configurations. Verify the part number changes dynamically, the price calculates correctly, and the Enterprise discount applies only when Enterprise is selected.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Attribute Container for Variable Line Items
sections.content:
Objective

Build an attribute container that allows a user to add a variable number of items during configuration, with per-row calculations and a product-level price aggregation formula.

Scenario

You are building a Managed IT Support configurable product. The customer specifies how many devices they want covered, with details per device.

Steps

  1. Create the following attributes centrally:
    • YourInitials_Device_Type (User Selection): Laptop, Desktop, Server, Printer
    • YourInitials_OS (User Selection): Windows 10, Windows 11, Linux, macOS
    • YourInitials_SLA (User Selection): Bronze, Silver, Gold
  2. Create a new attribute of type Container named YourInitials_Devices. Set it to use No Products.
  3. Add columns to the container:
    • Device_Type — String, Standalone, Dropdown (linked to YourInitials_Device_Type attribute). Required.
    • OS — String, Standalone, Dropdown (linked to YourInitials_OS). Required.
    • SLA — String, Standalone, Dropdown (linked to YourInitials_SLA). Required.
    • Monthly_Cost — Number, 2 decimals, Standalone, Label display, Read Only.
  4. Add a Calculation Formula to the Monthly_Cost column: Bronze = 29, Silver = 59, Gold = 99. Use:
    <*EVAL([IF](<*CTX(MyContainer.CurrentRow.Column(SLA).Get)*>="Gold"){99}[ENDIF][IF](<*CTX(MyContainer.CurrentRow.Column(SLA).Get)*>="Silver"){59}[ENDIF][IF](<*CTX(MyContainer.CurrentRow.Column(SLA).Get)*>="Bronze"){29}[ENDIF])*>
  5. Create a configurable product YourInitials_IT_Support. Assign the container attribute. Set Pricing Type to Custom Pricing, Base Price: 0, and add a Pricing Formula:
    <*CTX( Container(YourInitials_Devices).Sum(Monthly_Cost) )*>
  6. Set up the layout and test in Configuration Preview.

Validation

Add 3 rows: one Laptop/Windows 11/Gold, one Desktop/Windows 10/Bronze, one Server/Linux/Silver. Verify: row 1 = 99, row 2 = 29, row 3 = 59. Product total = 187. Add more rows and confirm the total updates dynamically.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — IronPython Script for Custom Table Lookup
sections.content:
Objective

Write an IronPython global script that reads data from a custom table and uses it to influence product configuration or pricing. This simulates a real-world data-driven configuration pattern.

Scenario

A custom table stores regional surcharges for the Cloud Hosting product. Instead of hard-coding regional prices on the attribute, the script looks them up from the table at configuration time.

Steps

Part A — Create the Custom Table

  1. Navigate to Setup → Product Catalog → Custom Tables.
  2. Create a table named YourInitials_Region_Surcharges.
  3. Add columns: Region_Code (String), Surcharge (Number, 2 decimals), Valid_From (Date), Valid_To (Date).
  4. Add rows:
    • EUW, 0, 2026-01-01, 2026-12-31
    • EUN, 10, 2026-01-01, 2026-12-31
    • USE, 20, 2026-01-01, 2026-12-31
    • USW, 25, 2026-01-01, 2026-12-31
    • APAC, 50, 2026-01-01, 2026-12-31

Part B — Write the Global Script

  1. Navigate to Setup → Develop → Global Scripts.
  2. Create a new script named YourInitials_GetRegionSurcharge.
  3. Write an IronPython function that:
    • Accepts a region code as a parameter.
    • Queries the custom table for a matching row where the current date falls within the validity period.
    • Returns the surcharge value (or 0 if no match is found).
  4. Add error handling (try/except) and logging so failures are visible in the script log.

Part C — Integrate with the Product

  1. On the Cloud Hosting configurable product, go to the Scripts tab.
  2. Assign a product-level script that calls the global function, passing the selected region's value code.
  3. Use the returned surcharge to adjust the displayed price or to populate a read-only attribute showing the surcharge amount.

Validation

Configure the product and select different regions. Verify that the surcharge displayed matches the custom table data. Change a surcharge value in the custom table, reconfigure the product, and confirm the new value is reflected — demonstrating data-driven logic without code changes.
